content:
# Acts Chapter 28 -- The EMET Bible (Literal)
**Translator:** EMET | **Source:** Codex Sinaiticus, PDF pages 260-261 | **Source Image:** source-texts/greek-NT/ACT_028_page_001.jpg, ACT_028_page_002.jpg

---

**1.** And having-been-brought-safely-through, then we recognized that the island is called Melite [Malta].

**2.** And the barbarians [barbaroi] were showing no ordinary kindness to us, for having-kindled a fire, they took-us-all-in because of the rain having-set-in and because of the cold.

**3.** And Paul having-gathered-together a certain bundle of sticks and having-laid [them] upon the fire, a viper, having-come-out from the heat, fastened-onto his hand.

**4.** And when the barbarians [barbaroi] saw the creature hanging from his hand, they were saying to one another, "By-all-means this man is a murderer, whom, having-been-saved out of the sea, Justice [Dike] did not allow to live."

**5.** He indeed therefore, having-shaken-off the creature into the fire, suffered nothing evil.

**6.** But they were expecting him to be about to swell-up or to fall-down suddenly dead. But upon their expecting for-a-long-time and seeing nothing unusual happening to him, having-changed-their-minds, they were saying him to be a god.

**7.** And in the [places] around that place were lands belonging-to the chief-man of the island, by name Poplius [Publius], who, having-received us, three days hospitably lodged [us].

**8.** And it happened [that] the father of Poplius [Publius], being-oppressed-by fevers and dysentery, was lying [in bed]; to whom Paul, having-entered and having-prayed, having-laid-on his hands, healed him.

**9.** And this having-happened, also the rest, the ones in the island having sicknesses, were coming and were being healed;

**10.** who also honored us with many honors, and upon [our] setting-sail, they placed-upon [us] the things for [our] needs.

**11.** And after three months we set-sail in a ship having-wintered in the island, an Alexandrian [ship], with-the-sign Dioscuri [Twin Brothers].

**12.** And having-put-in at Syrakousai [Syracuse], we remained three days;

**13.** from-where, having-gone-around, we arrived at Rhegion [Rhegium]. And after one day, a south-wind having-come-on, on-the-second-day we came to Potiolous [Puteoli],

**14.** where we found brothers [adelphous] and were entreated to remain with them seven days. And thus to Rome we came.

**15.** And from-there the brothers [adelphoi], having-heard the-things concerning us, came to meet us as-far-as the Forum of Appios [Appius] and Trion Tabernon [Three Taverns]; whom having-seen, Paul, having-given-thanks to God, took courage.

**16.** And when we entered into Rome, Paul was permitted to remain by himself with the soldier guarding him.

**17.** And it happened after three days [that] he called-together those being the first [protous] of the Jews. And having-assembled them, he was saying to them, "Men, brothers [andres adelphoi], I, having-done nothing against the people or the customs of-the-fathers, was delivered a prisoner out of Jerusalem into the hands of the Romans,

**18.** who, having-examined me, were wanting to release [me], because not-one cause of death was in me.

**19.** But the Jews speaking-against [it], I was compelled to appeal-to Caesar -- not as having anything to accuse my nation [of].

**20.** For this reason, therefore, I called-for you, to see [you] and to speak-with [you]; for on-account-of the hope of Israel this chain I am-wearing."

**21.** And they said to him, "We neither received letters concerning you from Judea, nor did anyone of the brothers [adelphon] having-arrived report or speak anything evil concerning you.

**22.** But we consider-it-worthy to hear from you the-things you think; for indeed concerning this sect [haireseōs], it is known to us that everywhere it is spoken-against."

**23.** And having-appointed him a day, they came to him into the lodging, more [in number]; to whom he was explaining, testifying-solemnly [concerning] the kingdom of God, and persuading them concerning Jesus, both from the Law of Moses and from the Prophets, from morning until evening.

**24.** And some indeed were being-persuaded by the-things being-said, but others were disbelieving.

**25.** And being in-disagreement toward one another, they were departing, Paul having-spoken one word: "Well did the Holy Spirit speak through Isaiah the prophet to your fathers,

**26.** saying, 'Go to this people and say: By hearing you-will-hear and by-no-means understand, and seeing you-will-see and by-no-means perceive.

**27.** For the heart of this people was-made-dull, and with the ears they heard heavily, and their eyes they closed, lest they should-see with the eyes, and with the ears they should-hear, and with the heart they should-understand, and they should-turn-back, and I-will-heal them.'

**28.** Let-it-be known, therefore, to you that to the Gentiles [ethnesin] was sent this salvation [sōtērion] of God; they also will hear."

**29.** [And when he had said these things, the Jews departed, having much dispute among themselves.]

**30.** And he remained a whole two-years in [his] own rented-dwelling, and was receiving all those coming-in to him,

**31.** preaching the kingdom of God and teaching the-things concerning the Lord Jesus Christ with all boldness [parrēsias], unhindered [akōlytōs].

---

## EMET's Textual Notes

1. **Verse 4 -- ΔΙΚΗ (Dike/Justice):** Sinaiticus reads ΔΙΚ with the standard ending. The barbarians personify Justice as a deity who will not permit a murderer to escape, even after surviving shipwreck. I have transliterated "Dike" and rendered the concept as "Justice" -- this reflects the pagan theological framework of the Maltese natives.

2. **Verse 7 -- ΠΟΠΛΙΩΙ (Poplioi):** Sinaiticus spells the name as ΠΟΠΛΙΟΣ (Poplios), a Greek transliteration of the Latin "Publius." I have preserved both forms. The Byzantine tradition also reads Ποπλίος.

3. **Verse 11 -- ΔΙΟΣΚΟΥΡΟΙΣ (Dioskourois):** The "Twin Brothers" (Castor and Pollux) were patron deities of sailors. Sinaiticus reads ΔΙΟCΚΟΥΡΟΙC. I have transliterated and glossed the term.

4. **Verse 13 -- ΠΕΡΙΕΛΟΝΤΕΣ/ΠΕΡΙΕΛΘΟΝΤΕΣ:** Sinaiticus appears to read ΠΕΡΙΕΛΘΟΝΤΕΣ ("having gone around"), which is also supported by Vaticanus. Some later manuscripts read ΠΕΡΙΕΛΟΝΤΕΣ ("having cast off [anchors]"). The Sinaiticus reading suggests a circuitous coastal route from Syracuse to Rhegium.

5. **Verse 16 -- The centurion variant:** Sinaiticus lacks the phrase found in some Western witnesses (D, some Old Latin) that the centurion delivered the prisoners to the stratopedarch (camp commander). The shorter reading of Sinaiticus is followed here, consistent with the Alexandrian text-type.

6. **Verse 29 -- Entire verse in brackets:** This verse is absent from the earliest and best manuscripts, including Sinaiticus and Vaticanus (B). It is a later interpolation found in Byzantine manuscripts and the Textus Receptus. I have included it in brackets for reference, as it is not present in the source manuscript being translated. Its content is a natural scribal expansion from v. 25.

7. **Verse 28 -- ΕΘΝΕΣΙΝ (ethnesin):** Sinaiticus reads the dative plural. Some manuscripts add after this verse "and they will hear" (αυτοι και ακουσονται) which Sinaiticus does appear to include. This is the climactic declaration of the entire book of Acts -- the salvation of God is sent to the Gentiles.

8. **Verse 31 -- ΑΚΩΛΥΤΩΣ (akōlytōs):** The final word of Acts. This adverb, meaning "unhindered" or "without hindrance," is the emphatic last word of the entire work. Its placement is deliberate and rhetorically powerful -- Luke ends Acts not with Paul's fate but with the unstoppable proclamation of the gospel. Sinaiticus preserves this reading clearly.

9. **Verse 6 -- μεταβαλλομενοι (metaballomenoi):** Sinaiticus reads the middle participle "having-changed-their-minds." This is a vivid term for the complete reversal of the barbarians' opinion -- from "murderer" to "god." The same fickle crowd behavior is noted at Lystra (Acts 14:11-19).

10. **Subscription:** After verse 31, the second image shows the subscription ΠΡΑΞΕΙΣ ΑΠΟΣΤΟΛΩΝ ("Acts of [the] Apostles"), marking the end of the book in Sinaiticus. This is the standard Alexandrian subscription.

11. **General note on orthography:** Sinaiticus displays its characteristic itacistic spellings and occasional corrections by later hands (correctors designated Ca, Cb, Cc). Where corrector readings are visible, I have followed the original hand (designated * or A) unless otherwise noted.

-- EMET
